schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch survey the whole reform as one picture dated to the eighteenth year, while Ellicott parses the name Josiah ('Jah healeth'), Bozkath in the lowland, and the queen-mother's early regency.
Gill computes that the eight-year-old king must have been born when Amon was but sixteen.
Henry reads the boy-king as the triumph of distinguishing grace, a chosen vessel grafted from a poisoned stock into the good olive.
Clarke ranks Josiah the best of the kings since David, who began, continued, and ended well.
Keil and Delitzsch stress the completeness of 'all the way of David' with no turning aside; Ellicott refers the phrase to the parallel testimony of 2 Chronicles 34:2.
Gill locates the king's 'right' first of all in the sphere of religious worship.
Henry makes the verse a text on sovereign grace, God keeping Josiah from both superstition and profaneness.

themes: did right in the LORD's sight

schools.summary: Gill fixes the eighteenth year as of the reign, not the age, and a late stage in a reform begun in Josiah's eighth and twelfth years.
Henry attributes the delay to opposition, urging that time lost in minority be redeemed by vigour.
Wesley glosses 'the scribe' as the secretary of state.
Ellicott defends the historicity of the officers named in Chronicles and distinguishes this Hilkiah from Jeremiah's father.
Keil and Delitzsch read the mandate as one long sentence closing only at v.8; Ellicott glosses 'sum' as reckoning up the amount (LXX 'seal up') and ties the door-keepers to 2 Kings 12:9.
Clarke reckons some ten years of unaudited offerings had accumulated since Josiah first restored the worship.
Gill places Hilkiah in his temple chamber and again rejects his identification with Jeremiah's father.
Gill marks the plain chain of delivery from priest and scribe to the overseers of the work.
Henry commends the gentle, voluntary collection laid out for the repair by competent hands.
Keil and Delitzsch read the clause as part of the unbroken royal command running to Hilkiah's announcement.
Gill distributes the labour between mending the woodwork and repairing the stone walls.
Henry reads the naming of the trades as testimony to a costly, public work of piety.
Keil and Delitzsch fold the carpenters, builders, and masons into the long sentence of the royal mandate.

themes: the scaffolding of a greater mending

schools.summary: Gill notes no audit was kept between the officers who paid and the overseers who received.
Henry praises the workmen's proven honesty while wishing, for others' satisfaction, that an account had still been kept.
Ellicott would render 'Howbeit there was' as 'Only let there be,' making the clause part of the king's own mandate.

debates.question: Does the verse report a fact or prescribe a policy of trust?

sides.position: a record of trust honoured — no reckoning was made because the men had proved faithful
a term of the royal mandate — let the money be handed over without strict reckoning

schools.summary: Clarke and Wesley take the book for the Mosaic autograph laid beside the ark (Deut. 31:26), hidden under the idolatrous kings and now found among the rubbish.
Henry sees the hand of God in the book's preservation, most likely the very copy laid up in the most holy place.
Gill sets the finding after Hilkiah's survey of the temple and its stores.
Ellicott, from the definite 'the book of the Torah,' argues for a known but long-disused collection of the law afterward shaped into the Pentateuch and Deuteronomy.

debates.question: What was the book of the law that Hilkiah found?

sides.position: the Mosaic autograph or original laid beside the ark, long hidden and now recovered
a known collection of Mosaic statutes, disused since Manasseh, later worked up into the Pentateuch and especially Deuteronomy

themes: the book of the law found

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch and Ellicott gloss 'gathered' as 'poured out' the money from chest into bags, and take 'the house' in the wider sense including the court.
Gill notes only that Shaphan brought the king word of his commission discharged.

Keil and Delitzsch make the verse the narrative's pivot and hear novelty in 'Hilkiah hath delivered me a book'; Ellicott doubts the whole was read, since 'all the words' is withheld till the next verse.
Gill marks that Shaphan 'further related' the matter of the book after the money.

themes: the curses of Leviticus and Deuteronomy
the word set before the throne

schools.summary: Henry reads the rent clothes as the rent heart of a king ashamed of his people's sin and afraid of God's wrath.
Gill presses the novelty of the law to the king, who had only abstracts and had reformed chiefly by abolishing idolatry.
Wesley thinks Josiah had likely read it before but was newly awakened by its solemn finding, and marvels at the providence that preserved the whole book of God.

debates.question: Was the book of the law new to Josiah, or known but neglected?

sides.position: effectively unknown — surviving only in abstracts, never read whole
known and probably read before, but quickened by its strange and seasonable finding

themes: was the law new or neglected

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch and Ellicott identify the five envoys — Ahikam the later protector of Jeremiah, Achbor, Shaphan, and Asahiah the king's aide — comparing the like embassy to Isaiah in 2 Kings 19:2.
Gill notes the Hilkiah charged is the high priest already named.

themes: officers who reappear in Judah's last days
a conscience that will not evade

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch infer the curses of Leviticus 26 and Deuteronomy 28 had been read; Ellicott renders 'seek ye Jehovah' and hears the king's anxiety whether any hope remains.
Henry reads two questions — what to do to turn the wrath, and what to provide for — urging magistrates to enquire for their people.
Wesley frames the double question of appeasing wrath and whether the curses must come without remedy.
Gill notes such inquiry was fittingly made of a prophet, like Jeremiah then five years in his office.

Keil and Delitzsch note nothing more is known of Huldah than her proven gift, dwelling in the Mishneh; they and Ellicott explain she was consulted because she was in Jerusalem while Jeremiah was at Anathoth.
Wesley likewise sets her nearness against Jeremiah's absence and explains 'the college'; Clarke turns the choice into a rebuke of an ignorant clergy.
Gill ranks Huldah with Miriam and Deborah, contrasting them with the counterfeit heathen Sibyls.

outliers.note: Extends the choice of Huldah into a polemic that pope, bishop, or priest may lack the true knowledge of God that a simple, godly woman possesses, citing Priestley on the spiritual parity of the sexes.

themes: the college in the second quarter

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch map the two-part oracle — doom to 'the man,' mercy to 'the king' — reading the general 'man' as deliberate.
Wesley hears in 'tell the man that sent you' that kings are but men to God, with whom is no respect of persons.
Gill notes simply that Huldah answered the king's messengers.

themes: tell the man that sent you
a herald in a higher court

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch read the enforcement of all the law's written curses; Ellicott corrects 'unto' to 'upon' and notes the book was read to, not by, the king.
Gill unfolds the twofold stroke of destruction to the place and captivity to its people.

themes: I will bring evil upon this place
all the words of the book
the fidelity of God to his word

schools.summary: Gill glosses the forsaking as abandoning God's worship, word, and ordinances.
Clarke hears a decree gone forth — long-suffering now useless, the wrath unquenchable — 'a dreadful message.'
Ellicott takes 'the works of their hands' for the idols they made (1 Kings 16:7) and links the unquenchable fire to Jeremiah and Amos.

themes: the darkest verse of the chapter

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch make the verse the seam of the oracle, turning from 'the man' to 'the king of Judah who sent to inquire,' from doom to a promised peace.
Gill marks the shift to what concerns the king in particular.

themes: but to the king of Judah
the turn from judgment to mercy

schools.summary: Henry: God distinguishes those who distinguish themselves — Josiah's tender heart, weeping in secret, was seen and every tear kept.
Clarke reads the answered prayer that the evil shall not fall in Josiah's lifetime; Wesley sums the marks of tenderness — trembling, grief, and fear of judgment.
Gill glosses the tender heart as soft wax that trembles at God's word.
Ellicott supplies the philology of 'tender' and compares Josiah's self-humbling with Ahab's.
Clarke argues Josiah was borne mortally wounded to Jerusalem and died there in peace (2 Chronicles 35:24); Wesley reads 'in peace' as a time of public peace and translation to everlasting peace.
Ellicott grants he was slain in battle (2 Kings 23:29) and limits 'in peace' by 'thine eyes shall not see the evil,' noting the variant 'thy graves.'
Gill takes the gathering to his godly fathers as a pledge of eternal life, not a bare natural death.

debates.question: How was Josiah 'gathered in peace' when he fell at Megiddo?

sides.position: he was mortally wounded but died at Jerusalem, at peace with God and land
he was slain in battle, but 'in peace' means spared from witnessing his people's ruin

themes: gathered to thy fathers in peace
taken from the evil to come
